Fitness & Health Math Financial Other
TDEE Lab
Calculator What is TDEE BMR vs TDEE

What is TDEE?

TDEE stands for Total Daily Energy Expenditure — the total number of calories your body burns in a 24-hour period. It accounts for everything: keeping your organs alive, digesting food, walking to work, and training at the gym.

How TDEE is Calculated

TDEE is the product of two values: your Basal Metabolic Rate (BMR) and your Activity Multiplier.

TDEE = BMR × Activity Multiplier

Your BMR is the number of calories your body needs to perform basic life-sustaining functions — breathing, circulation, cell production — while at complete rest. It is typically 60–70% of your total daily energy expenditure.

The Mifflin-St Jeor Equation

The most widely recommended formula for estimating BMR is the Mifflin-St Jeor equation, which the American Dietetic Association endorses as the most accurate for the general population:

For males:

BMR = 10 × weight(kg) + 6.25 × height(cm) − 5 × age + 5

For females:

BMR = 10 × weight(kg) + 6.25 × height(cm) − 5 × age − 161

Activity Multipliers

Once you have your BMR, multiply it by an activity factor based on your daily movement level:

Level Multiplier Description
Sedentary1.2Desk job, little exercise
Light1.375Exercise 1–3 days/week
Moderate1.55Exercise 3–5 days/week
Active1.725Hard exercise 6–7 days/week
Very Active1.9Athlete or physical job

Why TDEE Matters

Knowing your TDEE is the foundation of any nutrition plan. Whether your goal is weight loss, muscle gain, or maintenance, everything starts with understanding how many calories you burn.

  • Weight Loss: Eat below your TDEE. A deficit of 500 kcal/day results in approximately 0.5 kg (1 lb) of fat loss per week.
  • Weight Gain: Eat above your TDEE. A surplus of 500 kcal/day supports approximately 0.5 kg of weight gain per week.
  • Maintenance: Eat at your TDEE to maintain your current weight.

TDEE vs BMR

BMR is the calories your body burns at complete rest. TDEE includes BMR plus all the calories burned through daily activities and exercise. Think of BMR as your "engine idle" and TDEE as your "total fuel consumption."

Factors That Affect TDEE

Several factors influence your TDEE beyond just BMR and activity level:

  • Age: Metabolism slows approximately 1–2% per decade after age 20.
  • Body Composition: Muscle tissue burns more calories at rest than fat tissue.
  • Genetics: Some people naturally have faster or slower metabolisms.
  • Hormones: Thyroid hormones, cortisol, and insulin sensitivity all play roles.
  • Thermic Effect of Food (TEF): Your body burns calories digesting food — roughly 10% of total intake.

How to Use Your TDEE

The most practical approach: track your intake and weight for 2–3 weeks while eating consistently. If your weight stays stable, you're eating at your TDEE. If you gain or lose, adjust by 100–200 calories and reassess.

Online calculators like TDEE Lab provide a strong starting point, but individual variation means you should always treat the calculated value as an estimate and refine based on real-world results.

Frequently Asked Questions

How accurate is a TDEE calculator?
TDEE calculators based on the Mifflin-St Jeor equation are accurate within 5–10% for most individuals. The main sources of error come from estimating activity level and individual metabolic variation. For the most accurate results, use the calculator as a starting point and adjust based on 2–3 weeks of consistent tracking.
What is the best calorie deficit for weight loss?
A deficit of 300–500 calories below your TDEE is ideal for sustainable fat loss. This translates to approximately 0.25–0.5 kg (0.5–1 lb) of weight loss per week. Larger deficits (750–1100 kcal) can be used for faster results but should be approached with caution and ideally under professional guidance.
How many calories should I eat to build muscle?
To build muscle, eat 250–550 calories above your TDEE (a lean bulk). Combined with progressive resistance training, this supports muscle growth while minimizing fat gain. Pair with high protein intake (2.0–2.4g per kg of bodyweight) for optimal results.
Does muscle mass increase TDEE?
Yes. Muscle tissue is metabolically active and burns approximately 6–7 kcal per kg per day at rest, compared to fat which burns roughly 2 kcal per kg per day. While the difference is modest per kilogram, a significant increase in lean mass can meaningfully raise your BMR and overall TDEE.
